cards.definition: the main component of a computer that carries out instructions and processes data
the process the CPU repeats continuously: fetch an instruction from memory, decode it, then execute it
the part of the CPU that performs calculations and logical comparisons
the part of the CPU that coordinates all activities, directing the fetch-decode-execute cycle
tiny, very fast memory locations inside the CPU that temporarily store data being processed
a small, fast memory store inside or close to the CPU that holds frequently used data to speed up processing
the number of cycles the CPU performs per second, measured in GHz — higher means faster processing
individual processing units within a CPU — more cores allow more tasks to be processed simultaneously
a computer design where the CPU, memory, and input/output share a single bus to transfer data
a set of wires that transfers data between components of a computer

questions.question: What is the main job of the CPU?
options: To store all the computer's files permanently
To carry out instructions and process data
To display images on the screen
To control the keyboard and mouse
questions.correct_answer: To carry out instructions and process data
questions.explanation: The CPU is the central processing unit that executes instructions and handles all data processing in a computer.

questions.question: Which part of the CPU performs mathematical calculations?
options: The control unit
The cache memory
The ALU
The bus
questions.explanation: The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) is responsible for performing all calculations and logical comparisons in the CPU.

questions.question: What does the control unit do?
options: Stores frequently used data to speed up processing
Coordinates all CPU activities and directs the fetch-decode-execute cycle
Transfers data between computer components
Performs all mathematical calculations
questions.correct_answer: Coordinates all CPU activities and directs the fetch-decode-execute cycle
questions.explanation: The control unit is like the manager of the CPU, directing all operations and controlling the fetch-decode-execute cycle.

questions.question: What is cache memory?
options: The main memory that stores all your files and programs
A small, fast memory store inside or near the CPU that holds frequently used data
A permanent storage device like a hard drive
A type of cooling system for the CPU
questions.correct_answer: A small, fast memory store inside or near the CPU that holds frequently used data
questions.explanation: Cache is a small but very fast memory that stores data the CPU uses frequently, allowing quicker access than main memory.

questions.question: What is clock speed measured in?
options: Megabytes
GHz
Watts
Hertz only
questions.explanation: Clock speed is measured in GHz (gigahertz), which represents the number of cycles the CPU performs per second.

questions.question: What do CPU cores allow a computer to do?
options: Store more data permanently
Display graphics faster on screen
Process multiple tasks simultaneously
Cool down more efficiently
questions.explanation: More cores mean the CPU has more individual processing units, allowing it to work on multiple tasks at the same time.

questions.question: In the fetch-decode-execute cycle, what happens first?
options: The instruction is executed
The CPU decodes the instruction
An instruction is fetched from memory
The result is stored in cache
questions.correct_answer: An instruction is fetched from memory
questions.explanation: The fetch stage is the first step where the CPU retrieves an instruction from memory to begin the cycle.

questions.question: What is a bus in computer architecture?
options: A type of input device
A set of wires that transfers data between computer components
A cooling fan inside the CPU
A type of permanent storage
questions.correct_answer: A set of wires that transfers data between computer components
questions.explanation: A bus is a communication pathway made of wires that allows different parts of a computer to exchange data.

questions.question: What are registers?
options: Large storage devices that hold programs and files
Tiny, very fast memory locations inside the CPU that temporarily store data being processed
Wires that connect the CPU to the motherboard
Programs that control the monitor display
questions.correct_answer: Tiny, very fast memory locations inside the CPU that temporarily store data being processed
questions.explanation: Registers are the fastest type of memory in a computer, located directly in the CPU for immediate data access.

questions.question: Which architecture design has the CPU, memory, and input/output sharing a single bus?
options: Harvard architecture
Multi-core architecture
Von Neumann architecture
Cache architecture
questions.explanation: Von Neumann architecture is the most common computer design where all components share one communication bus for data transfer.

questions.question: The CPU is the component that stores all your files and documents permanently.
questions.explanation: The CPU processes data but doesn't store files permanently; permanent storage is handled by hard drives or SSDs.

questions.question: Higher clock speed means a CPU can perform more cycles per second.
questions.explanation: Clock speed measured in GHz directly indicates how many processing cycles occur per second; higher GHz means faster processing.

questions.question: A dual-core processor can perform two tasks at exactly the same time.
questions.explanation: A dual-core CPU has two separate processing units, allowing it to genuinely work on two tasks simultaneously.

questions.question: Cache memory is slower than the registers in the CPU.
questions.explanation: While cache is very fast, registers are located directly in the CPU and are even faster than cache memory.

questions.question: The control unit performs all mathematical calculations in the CPU.
questions.explanation: The ALU performs calculations; the control unit coordinates and directs all activities, not doing calculations itself.

questions.question: The fetch-decode-execute cycle repeats continuously while the computer is on.
questions.explanation: The CPU constantly loops through fetch-decode-execute cycles to process instructions as long as the computer is running.

questions.question: A bus is a physical component that can be seen and touched inside a computer.
questions.explanation: A bus consists of actual wires on the motherboard that physically connect components and transfer data.

questions.question: Registers can store the same amount of data as a hard drive.
questions.explanation: Registers are tiny and hold only small amounts of data temporarily; hard drives store vastly more data permanently.

questions.question: Von Neumann architecture uses separate buses for instructions and data.
questions.explanation: Von Neumann architecture uses a single shared bus; Harvard architecture uses separate buses for instructions and data.

questions.question: Cache memory helps speed up CPU processing by storing frequently accessed data nearby.
questions.explanation: Cache stores data the CPU uses often, reducing the time needed to fetch from slower main memory.

questions.question: The ________ is the part of the CPU that performs mathematical calculations and logical comparisons.
questions.explanation: The ALU (Arithmetic Logic Unit) is responsible for all calculations and logical operations in the CPU.

questions.question: The ________ cycle consists of three stages: fetch, decode, and execute.
questions.explanation: This is the fundamental cycle that the CPU repeats to process instructions from memory.

questions.question: ________ are tiny memory locations inside the CPU that temporarily store data during processing.
questions.explanation: Registers are the fastest memory in a computer, located directly within the CPU for immediate access.

questions.question: CPU ________ speed, measured in GHz, determines how many cycles the processor performs per second.
questions.explanation: Clock speed directly affects how fast a CPU can process instructions; higher clock speed means faster processing.

questions.question: A ________ is a set of wires that transfers data between different components of a computer.
questions.explanation: The bus acts as the communication highway in a computer, connecting the CPU, memory, and other components.

questions.question: ________ memory is a small, fast storage area inside or near the CPU that holds frequently used data.
questions.explanation: Cache speeds up processing by keeping frequently accessed data close to the CPU for quick retrieval.

questions.question: The ________ is responsible for coordinating all activities in the CPU and directing the fetch-decode-execute cycle.
questions.explanation: The control unit acts as the manager of the CPU, orchestrating all operations and ensuring proper execution of instructions.

questions.question: A CPU with multiple ________ can process more than one task at the same time.
questions.explanation: Each core is an independent processing unit; more cores enable parallel processing of multiple tasks simultaneously.

questions.question: ________ architecture is the most common computer design where the CPU, memory, and I/O share a single bus.
questions.explanation: Von Neumann architecture is the standard design used in most computers today, with components connected via a shared bus.

questions.question: The ________ is the main component of a computer that carries out instructions and processes all data.
questions.explanation: The CPU (Central Processing Unit) is the core component responsible for executing instructions and handling all computations.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: The part of the CPU that performs calculations and logical comparisons
The part of the CPU that coordinates all activities and directs the fetch-decode-execute cycle
A small, fast memory store inside or near the CPU that holds frequently used data
Tiny, very fast memory locations inside the CPU that temporarily store data being processed
A set of wires that transfers data between components of a computer

cards.definition: temporary, volatile memory that stores data and programs currently in use — contents lost when power is off
permanent, non-volatile memory that stores the boot instructions — cannot be changed and is not lost when power is off
memory that loses its contents when the power is switched off (e.g. RAM)
memory that keeps its contents when the power is switched off (e.g. ROM, SSD)
a section of secondary storage used as extra RAM when RAM is full — slower than real RAM
a type of non-volatile memory used in USB drives, SSDs, and memory cards — can be erased and rewritten
the program stored in ROM that runs when a computer starts up, checking hardware and loading the OS
carries the memory address of where data should be read from or written to
carries the actual data being transferred between the CPU and memory
a temporary storage area that holds data while it is being transferred between two components at different speeds

questions.question: What happens to data stored in RAM when you turn off a computer?
options: It is saved to the hard drive automatically
It is lost because RAM is volatile memory
It is transferred to ROM for safekeeping
It remains in RAM until you manually delete it
questions.correct_answer: It is lost because RAM is volatile memory
questions.explanation: RAM is volatile memory, which means it requires power to maintain its contents. When the power is switched off, all data in RAM is immediately lost.

questions.question: Which type of memory stores the boot instructions that run when a computer starts up?
options: RAM
Virtual memory
ROM
Flash memory
questions.explanation: ROM (Read Only Memory) is permanent, non-volatile memory that stores the BIOS and boot instructions. These instructions are needed every time the computer starts up and cannot be changed.

questions.question: What is virtual memory used for?
options: To store permanent files that should not be deleted
To act as temporary extra RAM when the main RAM is full
To replace the need for a processor
To store the operating system permanently
questions.correct_answer: To act as temporary extra RAM when the main RAM is full
questions.explanation: Virtual memory is a section of secondary storage (like a hard drive) that acts as extra RAM when the physical RAM becomes full. It is slower than real RAM but provides extra space when needed.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of non-volatile memory?
options: The CPU cache
RAM in a desktop computer
A USB drive using flash memory
The system RAM while the computer is running
questions.correct_answer: A USB drive using flash memory
questions.explanation: USB drives use flash memory, which is non-volatile. This means the data remains even when the USB drive is unplugged or the computer is turned off. RAM, on the other hand, is volatile.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the address bus in a computer?
options: To carry the actual data being transferred between components
To carry the memory address where data should be read from or written to
To control the speed of the processor
To store temporary data while waiting for the hard drive
questions.correct_answer: To carry the memory address where data should be read from or written to
questions.explanation: The address bus is responsible for carrying the memory address of where the CPU wants to read data from or write data to. It tells the memory system which location to access.

questions.question: A buffer is used to store data temporarily. Why might a buffer be needed between two computer components?
options: To prevent the processor from overheating
To allow data transfer between components that work at different speeds
To increase the storage capacity of RAM
To encrypt sensitive data during transfer
questions.correct_answer: To allow data transfer between components that work at different speeds
questions.explanation: A buffer temporarily holds data while it is being transferred between two components that operate at different speeds. This prevents data loss or slowing down when there is a mismatch in speeds.

questions.question: What is the main difference between flash memory and RAM?
options: Flash memory is faster than RAM
Flash memory is volatile, while RAM is non-volatile
Flash memory is non-volatile and can be erased and rewritten, while RAM is volatile
Flash memory is only used in older computers
questions.correct_answer: Flash memory is non-volatile and can be erased and rewritten, while RAM is volatile
questions.explanation: Flash memory is non-volatile, meaning it keeps its contents when power is off, and it can be erased and rewritten. RAM is volatile and loses its contents when powered off. Flash memory is used in SSDs and USB drives.

questions.question: What does the data bus do?
options: It carries memory addresses from the CPU to RAM
It carries the actual data being transferred between the CPU and memory
It controls when data can be written to memory
It stores data temporarily while waiting for the processor
questions.correct_answer: It carries the actual data being transferred between the CPU and memory
questions.explanation: The data bus carries the actual data being transferred between the CPU and memory. It works alongside the address bus, which carries the address of where the data should go.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the BIOS?
options: It is a program that runs continuously in the background while you use the computer
It is the operating system software that manages your files
It is a program stored in ROM that runs at startup and checks hardware before loading the OS
It is a type of memory that stores user files permanently
questions.correct_answer: It is a program stored in ROM that runs at startup and checks hardware before loading the OS
questions.explanation: The BIOS (Basic Input/Output System) is stored in ROM and automatically runs when the computer starts. It performs hardware checks and then loads the operating system.

questions.question: Why is it important that ROM is non-volatile memory?
options: Because it allows the computer to run faster
Because it means the boot instructions are preserved even when the power is off
Because it can store more data than RAM
Because it protects user files from being deleted accidentally
questions.correct_answer: Because it means the boot instructions are preserved even when the power is off
questions.explanation: ROM must be non-volatile because it stores the boot instructions that the computer needs every time it starts up. If ROM were volatile, these instructions would be lost when the power is off.

questions.question: RAM is volatile memory that loses its contents when the power is switched off.
questions.explanation: This is correct. RAM requires continuous power to maintain its contents. When you turn off the computer, all data in RAM is lost.

questions.question: ROM can be easily modified by users to change the boot instructions.
questions.explanation: ROM stands for 'Read Only Memory' — it cannot be changed by users. The boot instructions stored in ROM are permanent and cannot be modified.

questions.question: Virtual memory is faster than using physical RAM.
questions.explanation: Virtual memory is slower than physical RAM because it uses secondary storage (like a hard drive or SSD), which has slower access times than RAM.

questions.question: Flash memory is used in USB drives and SSDs.
questions.explanation: This is correct. Flash memory is a non-volatile memory type commonly used in USB drives, solid-state drives (SSDs), and memory cards.

questions.question: The address bus and data bus carry the same information.
questions.explanation: The address bus carries memory addresses, while the data bus carries the actual data. They serve different purposes in transferring information between components.

questions.question: Non-volatile memory keeps its contents when the power is switched off.
questions.explanation: This is correct. Non-volatile memory (like ROM, SSDs, and flash drives) retains its contents even when powered off, unlike volatile memory such as RAM.

questions.question: A buffer stores data permanently on the hard drive.
questions.explanation: A buffer is temporary storage used during data transfer between components at different speeds. It is not permanent storage on a hard drive.

questions.question: The BIOS is stored in ROM and runs automatically when the computer starts.
questions.explanation: This is correct. The BIOS is a program stored in ROM that automatically runs during the boot sequence to check hardware and load the operating system.

questions.question: RAM is used to store the operating system files permanently.
questions.explanation: RAM is temporary memory. The operating system files are stored on secondary storage (like a hard drive), but parts of the OS are loaded into RAM while the computer is running.

questions.question: Flash memory can be erased and rewritten multiple times.
questions.explanation: This is correct. Flash memory is designed to be erased and rewritten, which is why it is used in USB drives, SSDs, and memory cards where data needs to be modified.

questions.question: ________ is temporary, volatile memory that stores data and programs currently in use.
questions.explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is the correct answer. It is volatile memory that loses its contents when power is off.

questions.question: When RAM becomes full, the computer can use a section of secondary storage called ________ as extra memory.
questions.explanation: Virtual memory is a section of secondary storage used as extra RAM when the main RAM is full. It is slower than real RAM.

questions.question: The ________ carries the memory address of where data should be read from or written to.
questions.explanation: The address bus carries the memory address. It tells the memory system which location the CPU wants to access.

questions.question: ________ is permanent, non-volatile memory that stores the boot instructions and cannot be changed by users.
questions.explanation: ROM (Read Only Memory) is the correct answer. It is non-volatile and stores the BIOS and boot instructions permanently.

questions.question: Memory that loses its contents when the power is switched off is called ________ memory.
questions.explanation: Volatile memory is memory that loses its contents when power is off. RAM is an example of volatile memory.

questions.question: The ________ carries the actual data being transferred between the CPU and memory.
questions.explanation: The data bus carries the actual data. It works with the address bus to enable data transfer between the CPU and memory.

questions.question: ________ is a type of non-volatile memory used in USB drives, SSDs, and memory cards that can be erased and rewritten.
questions.explanation: Flash memory is the correct answer. It is non-volatile and commonly used in USB drives, SSDs, and memory cards.

questions.question: The ________ is the program stored in ROM that runs when a computer starts up, checking hardware and loading the OS.
questions.explanation: BIOS (Basic Input/Output System) is the correct answer. It is stored in ROM and automatically runs during the boot sequence.

questions.question: Memory that keeps its contents when the power is switched off is called ________ memory.
questions.explanation: Non-volatile memory keeps its contents even when powered off. Examples include ROM, SSDs, and flash drives.

questions.question: A ________ is a temporary storage area that holds data while it is being transferred between two components at different speeds.
questions.explanation: A buffer temporarily holds data during transfer between components that operate at different speeds, preventing data loss or slowdown.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: Temporary, volatile memory that stores data and programs currently in use
Permanent, non-volatile memory that stores the boot instructions
A section of secondary storage used as extra RAM when RAM is full
Non-volatile memory used in USB drives and SSDs that can be erased and rewritten
A program stored in ROM that runs at startup and checks hardware before loading the OS

cards.definition: permanent storage used to save data long-term, separate from RAM (e.g. HDD, SSD, optical disc)
a magnetic storage device that uses spinning platters to read and write data — high capacity, slower than SSD
a storage device with no moving parts that uses flash memory — faster and more durable than HDD but more expensive
storage that uses laser light to read/write data on discs (e.g. CD, DVD, Blu-ray)
storage that uses magnetism to store data (e.g. HDD, magnetic tape)
storing data on remote servers accessed via the internet — accessible from anywhere but requires internet connection
the maximum amount of data a storage device can hold, measured in GB or TB
how quickly data can be saved to or retrieved from a storage device
how resistant a storage device is to physical damage — SSDs are more durable than HDDs as they have no moving parts
the delay between requesting data and receiving it — lower latency means faster access

questions.question: What is secondary storage used for?
options: To store data temporarily while the computer is running
To permanently save data long-term, separate from RAM
To process instructions from the CPU
To display information on a screen
questions.correct_answer: To permanently save data long-term, separate from RAM
questions.explanation: Secondary storage is designed for long-term, permanent storage of data when the computer is switched off. This is different from RAM, which is temporary.

questions.question: Which storage device uses spinning platters to read and write data?
options: SSD
Optical disc
HDD
Cloud storage
questions.explanation: A Hard Disk Drive (HDD) uses magnetic spinning platters to store and retrieve data. This is a key characteristic that distinguishes it from SSDs.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of an SSD compared to an HDD?
options: It is cheaper to buy
It has no moving parts and is faster
It can store more data
It uses magnetic tape technology
questions.correct_answer: It has no moving parts and is faster
questions.explanation: SSDs use flash memory with no moving parts, making them faster and more durable than HDDs. However, they are typically more expensive.

questions.question: Which of these is an example of optical storage?
options: USB flash drive
Hard disk drive
DVD
Magnetic tape
questions.explanation: A DVD is optical storage because it uses laser light to read and write data on the disc. CDs and Blu-rays are also examples of optical storage.

questions.question: What technology does magnetic storage use to store data?
options: Laser light
Electrical circuits
Magnetism
Optical crystals
questions.explanation: Magnetic storage devices like HDDs and magnetic tape use magnetism to store data by magnetizing small areas on the storage medium.

questions.question: What is the main disadvantage of cloud storage?
options: It cannot store large files
It requires an internet connection to access data
It is slower than all other storage types
It cannot be accessed from multiple devices
questions.correct_answer: It requires an internet connection to access data
questions.explanation: While cloud storage is accessible from anywhere, you need an active internet connection to access your data. Without internet, you cannot retrieve your files.

questions.question: How is storage capacity typically measured?
options: In seconds and milliseconds
In watts and kilowatts
In GB or TB
In megapixels
questions.explanation: Storage capacity is measured in gigabytes (GB) or terabytes (TB), which indicate how much data a device can hold. For example, a 1TB HDD can store 1 terabyte of data.

questions.question: What does read/write speed refer to?
options: How loud the storage device is
How much the storage device costs
How quickly data can be saved to or retrieved from the device
How long the device lasts before breaking
questions.correct_answer: How quickly data can be saved to or retrieved from the device
questions.explanation: Read/write speed measures the performance of a storage device by showing how fast data can be written to it (write speed) or read from it (read speed).

questions.question: Why is an SSD more durable than an HDD?
options: It uses stronger magnetic materials
It is made of plastic instead of metal
It has no moving parts that can be damaged
It uses laser technology instead of magnets
questions.correct_answer: It has no moving parts that can be damaged
questions.explanation: SSDs have no moving parts, so they are more resistant to physical damage like drops or vibrations. HDDs have spinning platters that can be damaged if the device is dropped.

questions.question: What is latency in the context of storage devices?
options: The total amount of data stored on the device
The delay between requesting data and receiving it
The cost of the storage device
The temperature of the storage device
questions.correct_answer: The delay between requesting data and receiving it
questions.explanation: Latency is the time delay that occurs between when you request data and when the device actually provides it. Lower latency means faster data access.

questions.question: Secondary storage is designed to permanently save data even when the computer is switched off.
questions.explanation: Secondary storage is permanent storage, so your files remain saved when you turn off the computer, unlike RAM which loses all data when powered off.

questions.question: SSDs are cheaper to buy than HDDs with the same storage capacity.
questions.explanation: SSDs are more expensive than HDDs, even though they are faster and more durable. Cost is one of the main reasons people still use HDDs.

questions.question: Optical storage devices use laser light to read and write data.
questions.explanation: Optical storage (CDs, DVDs, Blu-rays) works by using a laser to read pits and bumps on the disc surface, which represent the stored data.

questions.question: Cloud storage can be accessed without an internet connection.
questions.explanation: Cloud storage requires an active internet connection because the data is stored on remote servers. Without internet access, you cannot retrieve your files.

questions.question: An HDD has moving parts that can be damaged by physical shock.
questions.explanation: HDDs have spinning platters and read/write heads that move, making them vulnerable to damage from drops or vibrations. This is why SSDs are more durable.

questions.question: Latency refers to the total amount of data a storage device can hold.
questions.explanation: Latency is the delay in accessing data, not the amount of data stored. The amount of data stored is called capacity.

questions.question: Magnetic tape is an example of magnetic storage technology.
questions.explanation: Magnetic tape uses magnetism to store data, just like HDDs. It is often used for long-term backup and archiving of large amounts of data.

questions.question: SSDs and HDDs have the same read/write speeds.
questions.explanation: SSDs are significantly faster than HDDs because they use flash memory instead of spinning platters. This makes SSDs the better choice for speed-critical applications.

questions.question: Cloud storage allows you to access your data from multiple devices via the internet.
questions.explanation: Cloud storage is accessible from anywhere with an internet connection, so you can access your files from different computers, tablets, or phones.

questions.question: A storage device with high capacity can hold more data than one with low capacity.
questions.explanation: Capacity directly measures how much data a device can store. A 2TB device can hold twice as much data as a 1TB device.

questions.question: A ________ is a permanent storage device used to save data long-term, separate from RAM.
questions.explanation: Secondary storage refers to any permanent storage outside of RAM, including HDDs, SSDs, optical discs, and cloud storage.

questions.question: An ________ uses spinning magnetic platters to store data and is slower than an SSD.
questions.explanation: HDD stands for Hard Disk Drive. The spinning platters are what make it slower than SSDs, which have no moving parts.

questions.question: An ________ is a fast storage device with no moving parts that uses flash memory.
questions.explanation: SSD stands for Solid State Drive. The lack of moving parts makes it more durable and faster than traditional hard drives.

questions.question: ________ storage uses laser light to read and write data on discs like CDs and DVDs.
questions.explanation: Optical storage technology uses a laser beam to read pits and bumps on the surface of discs, which represent the stored data.

questions.question: Storing data on remote servers that you can access via the internet is called ________ storage.
questions.explanation: Cloud storage is convenient because you can access your files from anywhere with an internet connection, but it requires an active internet connection.

questions.question: The ________ of a storage device is measured in GB or TB and tells you how much data it can hold.
questions.explanation: Capacity indicates the maximum amount of data a storage device can store. For example, a 500GB device can hold 500 gigabytes of data.

questions.question: ________ is how resistant a storage device is to physical damage, and SSDs are more durable than HDDs.
questions.explanation: SSDs are more durable because they have no moving parts that can be damaged by drops or vibrations, unlike HDDs with their spinning platters.

questions.question: The ________ of a storage device is the delay between requesting data and receiving it.
questions.explanation: Latency is measured in milliseconds. Lower latency is better because it means you get faster access to your data.

questions.question: ________ storage uses magnetism to store data and includes devices like HDDs and magnetic tape.
questions.explanation: Magnetic storage technology has been used for decades and is still common today because it offers good capacity at a reasonable cost.

questions.question: The ________ of a storage device measures how quickly data can be saved to or retrieved from it.
questions.explanation: Read/write speed is crucial for performance. SSDs typically have much faster read/write speeds than HDDs, making them better for fast data access.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: A storage device with no moving parts that uses flash memory and is faster than HDD
A magnetic storage device that uses spinning platters to read and write data
Storage that uses laser light to read and write data on discs
Storing data on remote servers accessed via the internet
The delay between requesting data and receiving it

cards.definition: a network connecting devices within a small geographical area such as a school or office building
a network connecting devices over a large geographical area — the internet is the largest WAN
a network where a central server provides services and resources to client devices
a network where all devices are equal and share resources directly with each other, with no central server
the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection per second, measured in Mbps
a small chunk of data that is sent across a network — large files are split into many packets
the method of sending data by breaking it into packets that can take different routes across a network
a unique numerical label assigned to every device on a network, used to identify and locate it
a unique hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface card — used to identify devices on a LAN
a set of agreed rules that determine how data is transmitted and received across a network

questions.question: What does LAN stand for?
options: Large Area Network
Local Area Network
Long-distance Access Network
Linked Automated Network
questions.explanation: LAN is an acronym for Local Area Network, which connects devices in a small geographical area like a school or office.

questions.question: Which of the following is the best example of a WAN?
options: Computers in a school library
Printers connected in an office
The internet connecting computers worldwide
Tablets in a single classroom
questions.explanation: A WAN (Wide Area Network) connects devices over a large geographical area. The internet is the largest WAN in existence.

questions.question: In a client-server network, what is the main role of the server?
options: To act as an equal device like all others
To provide services and resources to client devices
To prevent other devices from connecting
To share data only with one client at a time
questions.correct_answer: To provide services and resources to client devices
questions.explanation: A server in a client-server network is a central device that provides services and resources to all the client devices connected to it.

questions.question: What is a key advantage of a peer-to-peer network?
options: It requires a powerful central server
All devices are equal and share resources directly with each other
It is easier to manage than client-server networks
It prevents any device from accessing shared files
questions.correct_answer: All devices are equal and share resources directly with each other
questions.explanation: In a peer-to-peer network, all devices have equal status and can share resources directly with one another without needing a central server.

questions.question: What does bandwidth measure?
options: The physical width of network cables
The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted per second
The number of devices on a network
The age of the network infrastructure
questions.correct_answer: The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted per second
questions.explanation: Bandwidth refers to the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection per second, typically measured in Mbps.

questions.question: Why are large files split into packets when sent across a network?
options: To make them easier for humans to read
To reduce the file size permanently
To allow them to travel more efficiently across the network
To prevent other users from seeing the data
questions.correct_answer: To allow them to travel more efficiently across the network
questions.explanation: Files are split into small packets so they can travel independently and efficiently across a network, potentially taking different routes.

questions.question: What is packet switching?
options: Turning network packets on and off
Breaking data into packets that can take different routes across a network
Combining multiple packets into one larger packet
Preventing packets from leaving a network
questions.correct_answer: Breaking data into packets that can take different routes across a network
questions.explanation: Packet switching is the method of sending data by breaking it into small packets that can independently travel different routes across a network and then be reassembled.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an IP address?
options: To identify the speed of a device
To uniquely identify and locate a device on a network
To encrypt data being sent across the network
To determine what type of files a device can store
questions.correct_answer: To uniquely identify and locate a device on a network
questions.explanation: An IP address is a unique numerical label assigned to every device on a network, allowing the device to be identified and located for communication.

questions.question: What is a MAC address used for?
options: To connect to the internet
To identify devices on a LAN using a unique hardware address
To measure the bandwidth of a network
To encrypt all data sent over a network
questions.correct_answer: To identify devices on a LAN using a unique hardware address
questions.explanation: A MAC address is a unique hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface card, used to identify devices specifically on a local area network.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes a network protocol?
options: A physical wire that connects devices together
A set of agreed rules that determine how data is transmitted and received
A device that controls internet speed
A list of all devices connected to a network
questions.correct_answer: A set of agreed rules that determine how data is transmitted and received
questions.explanation: A protocol is a set of agreed rules that govern how data is transmitted and received across a network, ensuring devices can communicate properly.

questions.question: A LAN can only exist within a single room.
questions.explanation: A LAN connects devices within a small geographical area such as a school or office building, which can span multiple rooms or even multiple buildings in close proximity.

questions.question: The internet is the largest WAN in existence.
questions.explanation: A WAN connects devices over a large geographical area, and the internet is the largest WAN as it connects devices worldwide.

questions.question: In a peer-to-peer network, there must be a central server.
questions.explanation: A peer-to-peer network has no central server; instead, all devices are equal and share resources directly with each other.

questions.question: Bandwidth is measured in Mbps.
questions.explanation: Bandwidth is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted per second and is commonly measured in Megabits per second (Mbps).

questions.question: All packets sent from one device must travel along the same route to reach their destination.
questions.explanation: With packet switching, different packets can take different routes across the network and still be reassembled correctly at their destination.

questions.question: An IP address is permanently fixed to a device and never changes.
questions.explanation: While an IP address uniquely identifies a device on a network, it can change over time, especially with DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol).

questions.question: A MAC address is used to identify devices on a WAN.
questions.explanation: A MAC address is used to identify devices on a LAN (Local Area Network), not a WAN. IP addresses are used for identifying devices across wider networks.

questions.question: Without network protocols, devices would not be able to communicate effectively.
questions.explanation: Network protocols provide the agreed rules that allow devices to understand each other and communicate properly across a network.

questions.question: A client-server network requires every device to have equal processing power.
questions.explanation: In a client-server network, the server is typically more powerful than client devices, as it provides services and resources to many clients.

questions.question: Packet switching allows data to be sent more efficiently across a network.
questions.explanation: By breaking data into small packets that can take different routes, packet switching allows for more efficient use of network resources.

questions.question: A ________ is a network that connects devices within a small geographical area such as a school or office building.
questions.explanation: LAN stands for Local Area Network and is used for connecting devices in small, localized areas.

questions.question: In a ________ network, a central server provides services and resources to client devices.
questions.explanation: A client-server network has a central server that manages resources and provides services to all connected client devices.

questions.question: The ________ is the maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection per second.
questions.explanation: Bandwidth measures the data transmission capacity of a network connection and is typically measured in Mbps.

questions.question: Large files are split into small chunks called ________ before being sent across a network.
questions.explanation: Packets are small chunks of data that files are split into for efficient transmission across networks.

questions.question: ________ is the method of sending data by breaking it into packets that can take different routes across a network.
questions.explanation: Packet switching enables efficient data transmission by allowing packets to travel independently through different network paths.

questions.question: Every device on a network is assigned a unique ________ address to identify and locate it.
questions.explanation: An IP address is a unique numerical label that identifies each device on a network.

questions.question: A ________ address is a unique hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface card.
questions.explanation: A MAC address uniquely identifies a device on a LAN and is built into its network interface card.

questions.question: A ________ is a set of agreed rules that determine how data is transmitted and received across a network.
questions.explanation: Network protocols establish the rules and standards for how devices communicate and exchange data.

questions.question: In a ________ network, all devices are equal and share resources directly with each other, with no central server.
questions.explanation: A peer-to-peer network allows all devices to act as both clients and servers, sharing resources equally.

questions.question: A ________ is a network connecting devices over a large geographical area, with the internet being the largest example.
questions.explanation: WAN stands for Wide Area Network and connects devices across large distances, unlike a LAN.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: A network connecting devices within a small geographical area such as a school or office
A unique numerical label assigned to every device on a network for identification
A unique hardware address permanently assigned to a network interface card used on a LAN
The maximum amount of data that can be transmitted over a network connection per second
A set of agreed rules that determine how data is transmitted and received across a network

cards.definition: a device that forwards data packets between networks and connects a LAN to the internet
a device that connects multiple devices on a LAN and forwards data only to the intended recipient device
an older device that connects devices on a LAN and sends data to all devices — less efficient than a switch
hardware that allows a device to connect to a network, either wired or wireless
a network layout where all devices connect to a central switch or hub — most common in schools and offices
a network layout where all devices connect to a single central cable — failure of the cable breaks the whole network
a network layout where every device connects to every other device — very reliable but expensive to set up
a network connection using physical cables — faster and more secure than wireless
a network connection using radio waves — convenient and flexible but slower and less secure than wired
a system that translates human-readable domain names (e.g. google.com) into IP addresses

questions.question: What is the main purpose of a router?
options: To connect multiple devices on a LAN and forward data only to the intended device
To forward data packets between networks and connect a LAN to the internet
To translate domain names into IP addresses
To allow a device to connect to a network using cables
questions.correct_answer: To forward data packets between networks and connect a LAN to the internet
questions.explanation: A router's key job is connecting different networks together and providing internet access to local networks. This is different from a switch, which only works within a single network.

questions.question: Which device is more efficient at forwarding data on a LAN — a hub or a switch?
options: A hub, because it is older
A switch, because it sends data only to the intended device
They are equally efficient
A hub, because it sends data to all devices
questions.correct_answer: A switch, because it sends data only to the intended device
questions.explanation: A switch only sends data to the device that needs it, avoiding wasted data transmission. A hub sends data to all devices, which is wasteful and less efficient.

questions.question: What does a NIC (Network Interface Card) do?
options: It translates domain names into IP addresses
It forwards data packets between different networks
It allows a device to connect to a network, either wired or wireless
It connects multiple devices on a LAN together
questions.correct_answer: It allows a device to connect to a network, either wired or wireless
questions.explanation: A NIC is the hardware component that enables any device to connect to a network. Without a NIC, a device cannot access a network at all.

questions.question: Which network topology connects all devices to a single central cable?
options: Star topology
Mesh topology
Bus topology
Ring topology
questions.explanation: In a bus topology, all devices are connected to one central cable called the 'bus'. If this cable fails, the entire network stops working.

questions.question: What is a disadvantage of bus topology?
options: It is too expensive to set up
Every device must connect to a central switch
Failure of the single cable breaks the whole network
Devices cannot communicate with each other
questions.correct_answer: Failure of the single cable breaks the whole network
questions.explanation: Bus topology relies on one central cable. If that cable is damaged or fails, all devices lose network connection because there is no alternative path for data.

questions.question: Which network topology is most common in schools and offices?
options: Bus topology
Mesh topology
Star topology
Linear topology
questions.explanation: Star topology, where all devices connect to a central switch or hub, is the most common because it is reliable, easy to manage, and can handle many devices.

questions.question: Which statement about mesh topology is correct?
options: It is the cheapest topology to set up
Every device connects to a central hub
Every device connects to every other device
It uses a single central cable
questions.correct_answer: Every device connects to every other device
questions.explanation: In mesh topology, every device has connections to all other devices. This makes it very reliable because data can take many routes, but it is expensive because it needs many cables.

questions.question: What is an advantage of wired connections (Ethernet) compared to wireless?
options: They are more convenient and flexible
They are faster and more secure
They use radio waves instead of cables
They do not require a router
questions.correct_answer: They are faster and more secure
questions.explanation: Wired Ethernet connections use physical cables and provide faster speeds and better security. Wireless (Wi-Fi) is convenient but typically slower and more vulnerable to interference.

questions.question: What is the main advantage of Wi-Fi connections?
options: They are faster than wired connections
They are more secure than wired connections
They are convenient and flexible because devices can move around
They require no special hardware
questions.correct_answer: They are convenient and flexible because devices can move around
questions.explanation: Wi-Fi allows devices to connect without cables, giving users freedom to move around. However, it is typically slower and less secure than wired connections.

questions.question: What does DNS (Domain Name System) do?
options: It connects devices to a network using physical cables
It forwards data packets between different networks
It translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses
It sends data from a hub to all connected devices
questions.correct_answer: It translates human-readable domain names into IP addresses
questions.explanation: DNS converts easy-to-remember domain names like 'google.com' into the numerical IP addresses that computers use to find each other on the internet.

questions.question: A switch sends data to all devices on a network, just like a hub does.
questions.explanation: A switch is more intelligent than a hub. It sends data only to the device that needs it, making it more efficient. A hub sends to all devices.

questions.question: A router can connect a local network to the internet.
questions.explanation: One of the main functions of a router is to forward data between your local network (LAN) and the wider internet.

questions.question: A NIC is only needed for wired connections, not wireless ones.
questions.explanation: A NIC is needed for both wired and wireless connections. It is the hardware component that enables any device to connect to a network in either way.

questions.question: In star topology, if one cable fails, only one device loses connection to the network.
questions.explanation: In star topology, each device has its own connection to the central switch. If one cable breaks, only that device is affected; others continue working.

questions.question: Mesh topology is the cheapest type of network to set up.
questions.explanation: Mesh topology is actually the most expensive because every device must connect to every other device, requiring many cables and connections.

questions.question: Wi-Fi connections are generally faster than Ethernet (wired) connections.
questions.explanation: Wired Ethernet connections are typically faster than Wi-Fi. Wi-Fi is convenient but slower due to radio wave interference and other factors.

questions.question: DNS helps computers find websites by converting domain names to IP addresses.
questions.explanation: DNS translates human-readable names like 'bbc.co.uk' into IP addresses that computers can use to locate and access those websites.

questions.question: Bus topology is more reliable than star topology because it has no single point of failure.
questions.explanation: Bus topology actually has a major point of failure: the single central cable. If it breaks, the entire network stops. Star topology is more reliable.

questions.question: A hub is a device that forwards data only to the device that needs it.
questions.explanation: A hub sends data to all connected devices. A switch is the device that forwards data only to the intended recipient.

questions.question: Wireless connections are less secure than wired connections.
questions.explanation: Wireless signals can be intercepted more easily than signals in physical cables, making Wi-Fi generally less secure than Ethernet unless proper encryption is used.

questions.question: A ________ is a device that forwards data packets between networks and connects a LAN to the internet.
questions.explanation: A router is the device responsible for connecting local networks to the internet and managing traffic between different networks.

questions.question: A ________ forwards data only to the intended recipient device on a LAN, making it more efficient than a hub.
questions.explanation: A switch intelligently forwards data only to the device that needs it, unlike a hub which sends to all devices.

questions.question: In a ________ topology, all devices connect to a single central cable, and if that cable fails, the entire network stops working.
questions.explanation: Bus topology relies on one central cable. This is its main weakness: a single point of failure affects the whole network.

questions.question: ________ topology is the most common network layout in schools and offices, where all devices connect to a central switch.
questions.explanation: Star topology is widely used because it is reliable, easy to manage, and allows the network to work even if individual cables fail.

questions.question: In ________ topology, every device connects to every other device, making it very reliable but expensive to set up.
questions.explanation: Mesh topology provides excellent reliability because data can take many different routes between devices, but requires many cables and connections.

questions.question: A ________ is a hardware component that allows a device to connect to a network, either wired or wireless.
questions.explanation: A Network Interface Card (NIC) is essential hardware for any device that needs to connect to a network.

questions.question: ________ connections use physical cables and are faster and more secure than wireless connections.
questions.explanation: Wired connections, such as Ethernet, provide reliable, fast, and secure data transmission through physical cables.

questions.question: ________ connections use radio waves, making them convenient and flexible, but they are typically slower and less secure than wired connections.
questions.explanation: Wireless (Wi-Fi) connections allow devices to move freely without cables, but sacrifice speed and security compared to wired connections.

questions.question: ________ is a system that translates human-readable domain names like 'google.com' into IP addresses.
questions.explanation: The Domain Name System (DNS) converts easy-to-remember names into the numerical addresses computers need to find each other online.

questions.question: An older device that connects devices on a LAN and sends data to all devices is called a ________.
questions.explanation: A hub is an older networking device that broadcasts data to all connected devices, unlike the more efficient modern switch.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: A device that forwards data packets between networks and connects a LAN to the internet
A network layout where all devices connect to a central switch — most common in schools and offices
A network layout where every device connects to every other device — very reliable but expensive
A network connection using radio waves — convenient and flexible but slower and less secure than wired
A system that translates human-readable domain names like google.com into IP addresses

cards.definition: the protocol used to transfer web pages over the internet
a secure version of HTTP that encrypts data sent between a browser and a website
the set of protocols that governs how data is sent and received across the internet
a protocol used to transfer files between computers over a network
protocols used to send (SMTP) and receive (IMAP/POP) emails across networks
the full web address used to locate a resource on the internet (e.g. https://www.bbc.co.uk)
the language used to structure and display content on web pages
a system of interlinked web pages and resources accessed via the internet using HTTP
the global network of computers and servers connected together — the WWW runs on top of the internet
storing a website or application on a server so it can be accessed over the internet

questions.question: What does HTTP stand for?
options: HyperText Transfer Protocol
High Technology Text Processor
Home Terminal Transfer Protocol
Hyperlink Text Transmission Process
questions.explanation: HTTP is the standard protocol used to transfer web pages and other resources over the internet from web servers to your browser.

questions.question: Which protocol is a secure version of HTTP?
options: FTP
SMTP
HTTPS
TCP
questions.explanation: HTTPS adds encryption to HTTP, meaning data sent between your browser and a website is scrambled and protected from being read by others.

questions.question: What is TCP/IP?
options: A web browser used to access websites
The set of protocols that governs how data is sent and received across the internet
A type of computer virus that spreads over networks
A programming language used to create websites
questions.correct_answer: The set of protocols that governs how data is sent and received across the internet
questions.explanation: TCP/IP is the fundamental suite of protocols that allows all devices on the internet to communicate with each other reliably.

questions.question: What is FTP used for?
options: Sending emails between email servers
Encrypting data on websites
Transferring files between computers over a network
Hosting websites on the internet
questions.correct_answer: Transferring files between computers over a network
questions.explanation: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is specifically designed to transfer files between computers, and is commonly used by web developers to upload files to web servers.

questions.question: Which email protocol is used to send emails?
options: IMAP
POP
SMTP
FTP
questions.explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) handles sending emails from your email client to the email server, while IMAP and POP are used for receiving emails.

questions.question: What does URL stand for?
options: Universal Resource Locator
Uniform Resource Locator
Universal Reference Link
Unified Remote Location
questions.explanation: A URL is the full web address you type into your browser, such as https://www.bbc.co.uk, which identifies where a resource is located on the internet.

questions.question: What is HTML primarily used for?
options: Transferring files between computers
Encrypting data on the internet
Structuring and displaying content on web pages
Managing email on servers
questions.correct_answer: Structuring and displaying content on web pages
questions.explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is the standard language for creating web pages, using tags to structure content like headings, paragraphs, and links.

questions.question: What is the World Wide Web (WWW)?
options: The same thing as the internet
A system of interlinked web pages and resources accessed via the internet using HTTP
A protocol used to send files between computers
A type of web browser software
questions.correct_answer: A system of interlinked web pages and resources accessed via the internet using HTTP
questions.explanation: The WWW is an application that runs on top of the internet, allowing users to access interconnected web pages and resources through browsers.

questions.question: What is the internet?
options: A single large computer that stores all websites
The global network of computers and servers connected together
A software program used to view web pages
A type of electrical cable used to connect devices
questions.correct_answer: The global network of computers and servers connected together
questions.explanation: The internet is the physical and logical network infrastructure that connects billions of devices worldwide, allowing them to communicate and share data.

questions.question: What does hosting mean in the context of websites?
options: Designing the appearance of a website
Storing a website or application on a server so it can be accessed over the internet
Protecting a website from cyber attacks
Writing the code for a website
questions.correct_answer: Storing a website or application on a server so it can be accessed over the internet
questions.explanation: Web hosting means renting space on a web server where your website files are stored so people can access them over the internet 24/7.

questions.question: HTTP and HTTPS are exactly the same protocol with no differences.
questions.explanation: HTTPS is a secure version of HTTP that encrypts data, making it safer for transmitting sensitive information like passwords and payment details.

questions.question: TCP/IP is a single protocol used to transfer web pages.
questions.explanation: TCP/IP is actually a suite (collection) of multiple protocols that work together to govern how all data is sent and received across the internet.

questions.question: IMAP and POP are both protocols used for receiving emails.
questions.explanation: Both IMAP and POP are email protocols used to retrieve emails from servers, though they work in slightly different ways.

questions.question: A URL is the same thing as a domain name.
questions.explanation: A URL is the complete web address (like https://www.example.com/page), while a domain name is just the main part (like example.com).

questions.question: HTML is a programming language used to build websites.
questions.explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is a markup language used to structure and format content on web pages, though it's not a traditional programming language.

questions.question: The World Wide Web and the internet are completely the same thing.
questions.explanation: The internet is the underlying network infrastructure, while the WWW is an application that runs on top of the internet using HTTP/HTTPS protocols.

questions.question: FTP is commonly used to upload website files to web servers.
questions.explanation: FTP is frequently used by web developers and administrators to transfer website files from their local computer to the web hosting server.

questions.question: SMTP is used to receive emails from a mail server.
questions.explanation: SMTP is used to send emails, while IMAP and POP are used to receive emails from servers.

questions.question: Web hosting allows websites to be accessed over the internet.
questions.explanation: Web hosting services store website files on servers connected to the internet, making them accessible to users worldwide.

questions.question: HTTPS encrypts all data sent between a browser and a website.
questions.explanation: HTTPS uses encryption to scramble data during transmission, protecting sensitive information from being intercepted or read by unauthorized parties.

questions.question: ________ is the protocol used to transfer web pages over the internet.
questions.explanation: HTTP (HyperText Transfer Protocol) is the standard protocol for transferring web pages from web servers to browsers.

questions.question: ________ is a secure version of HTTP that encrypts data sent between a browser and website.
questions.explanation: HTTPS adds encryption to HTTP, ensuring data transmitted between your browser and a website cannot be easily intercepted.

questions.question: The ________ is the global network of computers and servers connected together that the WWW runs on top of.
questions.explanation: The internet is the underlying infrastructure of connected computers and servers that enables all networked communication worldwide.

questions.question: A ________ is the full web address used to locate a resource on the internet, such as https://www.bbc.co.uk.
questions.explanation: A URL (Uniform Resource Locator) is the complete address you enter in your browser to access a specific web page or resource.

questions.question: ________ is the language used to structure and display content on web pages.
questions.explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) uses tags to structure elements like headings, paragraphs, images, and links on web pages.

questions.question: ________ is a protocol used to transfer files between computers over a network.
questions.explanation: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is specifically designed for uploading and downloading files between computers on a network.

questions.question: ________ is the protocol used to send emails across networks.
questions.explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) handles the sending of emails from your email client to the email server.

questions.question: The ________ is a system of interlinked web pages and resources accessed via the internet using HTTP.
questions.explanation: The WWW consists of all the web pages and resources connected together that you can access through web browsers.

questions.question: ________ means storing a website or application on a server so it can be accessed over the internet.
questions.explanation: Web hosting services provide servers where website files are stored and kept online for users to access.

questions.question: ________ is the set of protocols that governs how data is sent and received across the internet.
questions.explanation: TCP/IP is the fundamental suite of protocols that enables all devices on the internet to communicate with each other reliably.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: A secure version of HTTP that encrypts data
A protocol used to transfer files between computers
The language used to structure content on web pages
The full web address used to locate a resource on the internet
Storing a website on a server so it can be accessed over the internet

cards.definition: any software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a computer system
malware that attaches itself to files and spreads to other files, often causing damage
malware that replicates itself across a network without needing to attach to a file
malware disguised as legitimate software that gives an attacker access to a system
malware that encrypts a victim's files and demands payment to restore access
a social engineering attack using fake emails or websites to trick users into revealing sensitive information
an attack that systematically tries every possible password combination until the correct one is found
an attack where malicious SQL code is inserted into a web form to access or manipulate a database
an attack that floods a server with traffic to make it unavailable to legitimate users
manipulating people into revealing confidential information or performing actions that compromise security

questions.question: What is malware?
options: Any software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a computer system
A type of antivirus program that protects your files
A network connection that is very slow
A backup system for your computer
questions.correct_answer: Any software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a computer system
questions.explanation: Malware is the umbrella term for any harmful software, including viruses, worms, trojans, and ransomware. The other options describe different things entirely.

questions.question: Which type of malware attaches itself to files and spreads to other files?
options: Worm
Virus
Ransomware
Trojan
questions.explanation: A virus needs to attach to existing files to spread, unlike a worm which can replicate itself independently across networks.

questions.question: What is the main characteristic of a worm?
options: It needs to attach to a file to spread
It encrypts your files and demands money
It replicates itself across a network without attaching to files
It disguises itself as legitimate software
questions.correct_answer: It replicates itself across a network without attaching to files
questions.explanation: Worms are self-replicating and can spread independently through networks, making them different from viruses which need file attachment.

questions.question: How does a trojan work?
options: It encrypts all your personal files
It replicates across network connections
It is disguised as legitimate software to trick users into installing it
It sends fake emails to steal passwords
questions.correct_answer: It is disguised as legitimate software to trick users into installing it
questions.explanation: Trojans deceive users by pretending to be legitimate programs, allowing attackers to gain access once installed. They don't self-replicate like worms.

questions.question: What does ransomware do?
options: It slowly damages your computer hardware
It encrypts a victim's files and demands payment to restore access
It spreads copies of itself to other computers
It monitors your internet activity
questions.correct_answer: It encrypts a victim's files and demands payment to restore access
questions.explanation: Ransomware is a type of malware specifically designed to lock users out of their files and extort money by promising to restore access.

questions.question: Phishing attacks typically use what method to trick users?
options: Installing hidden programs on computers
Fake emails or websites to trick users into revealing sensitive information
Flooding a server with too much traffic
Testing every possible password combination
questions.correct_answer: Fake emails or websites to trick users into revealing sensitive information
questions.explanation: Phishing is a social engineering attack that relies on deception through fraudulent emails and websites to obtain sensitive data.

questions.question: What is a brute force attack?
options: Sending many fake emails to overwhelm a server
Systematically trying every possible password combination until the correct one is found
Disguising malware as a legitimate program
Using social engineering to manipulate people
questions.correct_answer: Systematically trying every possible password combination until the correct one is found
questions.explanation: A brute force attack uses trial and error to crack passwords, testing combinations methodically until finding the correct one.

questions.question: How does SQL injection work?
options: It floods a website with thousands of requests
It sends fake login emails to users
Malicious SQL code is inserted into a web form to access or manipulate a database
It disguises itself as antivirus software
questions.correct_answer: Malicious SQL code is inserted into a web form to access or manipulate a database
questions.explanation: SQL injection exploits vulnerabilities in web forms by inserting harmful database commands that can steal or modify data.

questions.question: What is the purpose of a Denial of Service (DoS) attack?
options: To steal customer passwords
To encrypt files and demand ransom
To flood a server with traffic to make it unavailable to legitimate users
To trick users into clicking malicious links
questions.correct_answer: To flood a server with traffic to make it unavailable to legitimate users
questions.explanation: A DoS attack works by overwhelming a server with so much traffic that it cannot serve legitimate users, causing service disruption.

questions.question: Which attack method relies on manipulating people rather than exploiting technical vulnerabilities?
options: SQL injection
Brute force attack
Social engineering
DoS attack
questions.explanation: Social engineering manipulates people into revealing confidential information or performing actions that compromise security, rather than targeting technical systems directly.

questions.question: A virus can spread without attaching to a file.
questions.explanation: Viruses by definition need to attach to existing files to spread. Worms are the malware that can replicate independently without file attachment.

questions.question: Phishing is a type of social engineering attack.
questions.explanation: Phishing uses deception and psychological manipulation to trick users into revealing information, making it a form of social engineering.

questions.question: Ransomware allows attackers to delete files permanently from your computer.
questions.explanation: Ransomware encrypts files (locks them) rather than deleting them. The attacker can decrypt them if payment is made, though there's no guarantee.

questions.question: A trojan horse needs to attach to existing files to replicate itself.
questions.explanation: Trojans don't replicate; they work by deceiving users into installing them as legitimate software to gain unauthorised access to a system.

questions.question: Brute force attacks can be successful against weak passwords.
questions.explanation: Brute force attacks try all possible combinations systematically, so weak passwords with fewer combinations can be cracked quickly.

questions.question: SQL injection only affects email systems.
questions.explanation: SQL injection specifically targets web forms connected to databases, not email systems. It can affect any website that uses databases.

questions.question: A DoS attack is always a criminal act that can result in legal consequences.
questions.explanation: DoS attacks are illegal in most countries as they disrupt services and cause financial loss to organisations.

questions.question: Worms and viruses spread in exactly the same way.
questions.explanation: Viruses need to attach to files to spread, while worms can replicate and spread independently across networks without file attachment.

questions.question: Malware is software designed to benefit the user.
questions.explanation: Malware is defined as software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access — it harms rather than benefits users.

questions.question: Social engineering attacks can be prevented entirely by using firewalls.
questions.explanation: Social engineering attacks target people, not technical systems, so firewalls cannot prevent them. User awareness and training are more effective defences.

questions.question: ________ is malware that disguises itself as legitimate software to trick users into installing it.
questions.explanation: A trojan works by deceiving users into installing what appears to be legitimate software, giving attackers access once installed.

questions.question: A ________ attack systematically tries every possible password combination until the correct one is found.
questions.explanation: Brute force attacks use trial and error methodology to crack passwords by testing combinations methodically.

questions.question: ________ is a social engineering attack using fake emails or websites to trick users into revealing sensitive information.
questions.explanation: Phishing attacks use deception through fraudulent emails and websites designed to look like legitimate sources to steal information.

questions.question: A ________ replicates itself across a network without needing to attach to a file.
questions.explanation: Worms are self-replicating malware that can spread independently across networks, unlike viruses which need file attachment.

questions.question: ________ is malware that encrypts a victim's files and demands payment to restore access.
questions.explanation: Ransomware locks users out of their files through encryption and extorts money by promising to restore access.

questions.question: In a ________ attack, malicious SQL code is inserted into a web form to access or manipulate a database.
questions.explanation: SQL injection exploits vulnerabilities in web forms by inserting harmful database commands to steal or modify data.

questions.question: A ________ attack floods a server with traffic to make it unavailable to legitimate users.
questions.explanation: A Denial of Service attack works by overwhelming servers with excessive traffic, preventing legitimate users from accessing services.

questions.question: A ________ is malware that attaches itself to files and spreads to other files, often causing damage.
questions.explanation: Viruses spread by attaching to existing files, unlike worms which can replicate independently across networks.

questions.question: ________ is the term for any software designed to damage, disrupt, or gain unauthorised access to a computer system.
questions.explanation: Malware is the umbrella term covering all harmful software including viruses, worms, trojans, and ransomware.

questions.question: ________ involves manipulating people into revealing confidential information or performing actions that compromise security.
questions.explanation: Social engineering targets human psychology and trust rather than technical vulnerabilities to compromise security.

questions.question: Match each term to its correct description.

options.match: Malware that attaches to files and spreads to other files
Malware that replicates itself across a network without needing file attachment
Malware that encrypts files and demands payment to restore access
Malware disguised as legitimate software that gives attackers access
Social engineering attack using fake emails or websites to steal information

cards.definition: software or hardware that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules
the process of converting data into a coded form so only authorised parties can read it
the process of verifying a user's identity before granting access to a system
a security process requiring two different forms of verification (e.g. password + SMS code)
software that detects, quarantines, and removes malicious programs from a computer
an authorised simulated attack on a system to find and fix security vulnerabilities before real attackers do
restricting who can access certain data or systems, using usernames, passwords, and permission levels
protocols that encrypt data between a browser and a server, shown by the padlock icon in a browser
a set of rules governing acceptable use of a network — e.g. not sharing passwords, not installing unauthorised software
protecting hardware from physical access — e.g. locked server rooms, CCTV, cable locks

questions.question: What is a firewall?
options: A wall made of fire to protect servers
Software or hardware that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules
A type of antivirus program
A physical lock on a server room door
questions.correct_answer: Software or hardware that monitors and controls incoming and outgoing network traffic based on security rules
questions.explanation: A firewall is a security tool that acts as a barrier between networks, checking all traffic against a set of security rules to block threats.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes encryption?
options: Making passwords longer and more complicated
The process of converting data into a coded form so only authorised parties can read it
